sentence: Subjective:

The patient, a 54-year-old male, presents with a chronic history of stabbing, shooting pain in the right cheek bone area of his face, which he describes as excruciating and lasting from a few minutes to an hour.
He reports that the pain started about 2-3 months ago and has been ongoing.
He denies any sensory loss, numbness, or tingling in the affected area, but does report that the pain is triggered by certain temperatures, wind, and cold.
He has tried ibuprofen, Motrin, and Tiger Balm without relief.
His family doctor referred him to a neurosurgeon, who recommended that he see me.
The patient has a history of occasional headaches but no migraines or brain tumors.
He rates the pain as a 10 out of 10 in severity.
He has no history of multiple sclerosis.
Past medical history:
The patient has no significant past medical history, but he takes Tylenol occasionally for headaches.
Review of systems:
The patient denies any symptoms in other organs that are relevant to his initial presentation.
Current medications:
Tylenol (dose not specified) as needed for headaches.
Objective:
Vital signs:
Blood pressure: Not mentioned
Pulse rate: Not mentioned
Oxygen saturation: Not mentioned
Temperature: Not mentioned
Physical exam:
The patient follows my finger in both directions, has a symmetric smile, and can hear equally on both sides of his face.
The patient reports a little bit of numbness on his right side when I run cotton tape along the side of his face.
The patient reports that the left side of his face feels sharp when I poke it with a needle, while the right side feels dull.
Test Results:
MRI: Shows a small blood vessel abutting and perhaps pinching the trigeminal nerve.
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has trigeminal neuralgia, a condition caused by nerve compression from a small blood vessel.
Problem list: Trigeminal neuralgia; active.
Plan:
I prescribed Tegretol for the patient to reduce the excitability of the nerve and alleviate the pain.
We will monitor the patient's response to the medication and adjust the dosage as needed.
If the patient does not respond to the medication, we will discuss surgical options, including decompression of the nerve.
We will also perform genetic testing to ensure the patient is a good candidate for the medication.
Follow-up:
The patient will follow up in one month to assess the effectiveness of the medication and adjust the treatment plan as needed.
